ai_summary: Elon Musk announces that SpaceX's Falcon 9 booster has completed its 34th flight, demonstrating the company's progress toward rocket reusability—a core business objective for reducing launch costs and increasing flight cadence. This milestone exemplifies SpaceX's engineering execution in manufacturing and operational scaling, directly supporting the company's mission to make space transportation more economical and frequent.

text:
Tesla is making a big investment in Japan with service & Superchargers. 

Many of the parts in Teslas are made in Japan. Panasonic has been our biggest strategic supplier over the past 2 decades. https://t.co/XkwdUIPeKy
summary: Tesla is making a big investment in Japan with service & Superchargers. Many of the parts in Teslas are made in Japan. Panasonic has been our biggest strategic supplier over the past 2 decades.
ai_summary: Elon Musk announces Tesla's strategic investment in Japan focused on expanding service centers and Supercharger networks, while highlighting the critical role of Japanese suppliers—particularly Panasonic—in Tesla's manufacturing ecosystem over the past two decades. This illustrates a key business lesson: long-term supply chain partnerships with reliable vendors are foundational to scaling production, and geographic expansion requires simultaneous investment in customer-facing infrastructure (service/charging) to support market growth.

text: 34th launch & landing of the same rocket https://t.co/OK4xGVFSs6
summary: 34th launch & landing of the same rocket
ai_summary: Elon Musk announces SpaceX's 34th successful launch and landing of the same reusable rocket, demonstrating the company's progress in rocket reusability—a critical cost-reduction strategy for space operations. This milestone reflects SpaceX's engineering execution in achieving reliable, repeated use of orbital-class rockets, directly supporting the company's mission to reduce launch costs and increase launch cadence for Starlink deployment and other commercial/government missions.

34th launch & landing of the same rocket https://t.co/OK4xGVFSs6

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) March 30, 2026

summary: In 4 or 5 years, there will be a launch every hour
ai_summary: Elon Musk states that SpaceX will achieve launch cadence of one rocket per hour within 4-5 years, reflecting the company's ambitious scaling goals for Starship operations. This represents a significant engineering and operational milestone that would fundamentally transform commercial space access, demonstrating SpaceX's strategy to dramatically increase launch frequency through reusable rocket technology and manufacturing efficiency improvements.

text: Neuralink enables people with ALS to speak again https://t.co/jGLiUjRT1R
summary: Neuralink enables people with ALS to speak again
ai_summary: Elon Musk announced that Neuralink has successfully enabled a person with ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) to speak again through its brain-computer interface technology. This demonstrates a major product milestone for Neuralink—moving from animal testing and initial human trials to achieving functional restoration of communication capabilities in paralyzed patients, representing significant progress in the company's core mission of creating implantable neural interfaces.

text:
Indeed, it was *because* I was not from the aerospace industry that SpaceX made such radical breakthroughs. Same for Tesla. 

Those in the industry would have if they could have. https://t.co/MYzMLrpOhh
summary: Indeed, it was *because* I was not from the aerospace industry that SpaceX made such radical breakthroughs. Same for Tesla. Those in the industry would have if they could have.
ai_summary: Elon Musk argues that his outsider status to aerospace and automotive industries enabled SpaceX and Tesla to achieve radical breakthroughs that established players could have but didn't pursue. The insight is that industry expertise can create cognitive constraints and risk-aversion—being unfamiliar with 'how things are done' can be a competitive advantage when challenging entrenched business models and technical assumptions.
